caveats: a: The occupation source covers all gasoline stations, not specifically 457110 establishments with substantive repair operations.
a: Task sources do not supply industry wage weights or current automation penetration.
a: The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio is a low-quality retail-sector ancestor and may dilute the labor intensity of repair service.
rho: No implementation study specific to gasoline-station repair businesses was found.
rho: The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects and applies only to exposed tasks in a.
e: Public data do not report the share of 457110 firms with substantive automotive repair operations.
e: Repeat fuel purchase and fuel-card billing are treated as recurring merchandise, not an outsourced service.
e: Repair-heavy gasoline stations without convenience stores may instead be classified in 457120, which depresses eligibility in this code.
s5: The owner-age source is all-industry and data year 2018.
s5: No qualifying-transfer denominator for the narrow eligible subset was found.
s5: A real-estate sale, fuel-brand change, or lease transfer is not necessarily a control transfer of the service operating company.
q: No source directly measures AI cost pass-through in station-based automotive repair.
q: Retention differs between walk-in consumers, fleets, warranty work, and insurer-influenced repairs.
q: The range excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty.
d5: Occupational employment is not direct service quantity and covers all automotive repair settings.
d5: National gasoline consumption is not a direct measure of demand for station-based repair.
d5: Powertrain transition, vehicle age, miles traveled, and local station closures may move in offsetting directions.
o: The operator may use centralized or remote support, so this estimate does not imply current store staffing remains unchanged.
o: Simple diagnostics and software resets are more substitutable than mechanical inspection and repair.
o: The estimate applies to year-five service quantity after d5, not fuel or grocery transaction volume.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Included firms combine high-frequency retail fuel and convenience sales with externally purchased repeat automotive service, typically billed as flat-rate or actual-time labor plus parts and sometimes through fleet-account arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Stations limited to fuel, grocery, prepared-food, lottery, tobacco, or other merchandise sales; fuel-card accounts without a supplied service; standalone charging sites; incidental air or vacuum machines; captive fleet facilities; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market gasoline stations with convenience stores that also provide a substantive repeat automotive inspection, maintenance, and repair service to external vehicle owners or fleets.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for all gasoline stations rather than the repair-service subset, while O*NET and BLS describe cashier and automotive-technician duties rather than wage-weighted task time. I map payment, reconciliation, customer Q&A, scheduling, estimate drafting, diagnostic assistance, inventory, and routine records to AI exposure, then discount for stocking, food handling, forecourt safety, inspection, wrench work, test driving, and physical repairs. Because included stations likely employ more mechanics than the parent, the cashier-heavy parent mix may overstate exposure; conversely, it omits some automatable service-office work.
caveats: The occupation source covers all gasoline stations, not specifically 457110 establishments with substantive repair operations.
Task sources do not supply industry wage weights or current automation penetration.
The supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio is a low-quality retail-sector ancestor and may dilute the labor intensity of repair service.
a.change_evidence: Payroll and time-on-task data for qualifying station-repair businesses, split among store, forecourt, service writer, technician, parts, bookkeeping, and management work and net of existing pay-at-pump, POS, and diagnostic automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Gasoline-station employment is dominated by cashiers, whose transaction and information tasks are already technology-susceptible, while the qualifying repair service involves computer-assisted diagnosis but extensive physical inspection and repair. The resulting not-yet-automated wage opportunity is material but clearly a minority.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of automotive service technicians across all repair settings to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034, while EIA reports gasoline consumption fell 1% in 2025 and expects declines through 2027, and DOE finds battery-electric vehicles have substantially lower scheduled maintenance costs than conventional vehicles. I convert these adjacent signals into a near-flat five-year constant-quality repair-service quantity for station-based operators, with downside for declining fuel visits and EV maintenance and upside from the large aging combustion-vehicle stock. Technician employment can understate volume if productivity rises and overstate this subset if work migrates to dealerships or specialized shops.
caveats: Occupational employment is not direct service quantity and covers all automotive repair settings.
National gasoline consumption is not a direct measure of demand for station-based repair.
Powertrain transition, vehicle age, miles traveled, and local station closures may move in offsetting directions.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted repair orders and standardized labor operations per qualifying 457110 site for five years, segmented by powertrain, fleet versus consumer, and same-store location, would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: The existing gasoline vehicle fleet will still require repair over five years, supporting a near-flat base, but improving fuel economy reduces station visits and all-electric vehicles require less routine maintenance. Convenience-store traffic, vehicle age, repair complexity, and technician demand cushion the transition, while the co-located service model may lose share to specialized repair networks.

caveats: Public data do not report the share of 457110 firms with substantive automotive repair operations.
Repeat fuel purchase and fuel-card billing are treated as recurring merchandise, not an outsourced service.
Repair-heavy gasoline stations without convenience stores may instead be classified in 457120, which depresses eligibility in this code.
e.change_evidence: A 457110 establishment census reporting repair bays, technician payroll, repair revenue, repeat or fleet-service customers, and LMM EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official industry is primarily retail fuel plus a limited grocery line and says establishments may also provide automotive repair. Fuel and merchandise transactions are not outsourced services, so most firms fail the fixed lens; eligibility is confined to the small, unmeasured minority where repeat repair and maintenance is a substantive operating activity rather than a trivial amenity.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The operator may use centralized or remote support, so this estimate does not imply current store staffing remains unchanged.
Simple diagnostics and software resets are more substitutable than mechanical inspection and repair.
The estimate applies to year-five service quantity after d5, not fuel or grocery transaction volume.
o.change_evidence: Field evidence from highly automated repair sites measuring the share of complete repair orders delivered without on-site skilled labor or accountable human review, together with safety, rework, insurance, and regulatory outcomes, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers can self-pay at pumps and self-check out merchandise, and software can handle booking, reminders, basic triage, quotes, and records. The qualifying service still requires an accountable operator to inspect vehicles, authorize safe work, control parts, perform physical repairs, test outcomes, handle hazardous materials and exceptions, and stand behind workmanship; AI is more likely to augment than eliminate that entity within five years.

caveats: No source directly measures AI cost pass-through in station-based automotive repair.
Retention differs between walk-in consumers, fleets, warranty work, and insurer-influenced repairs.
The range excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Job-level data linking documented automation to billed hours, posted labor rates, realized technician and service-writer hours, discounts, fleet-contract repricing, customer retention, and contribution margin over subsequent years would materially change q.
q.rationale: Repair labor is commonly priced using a flat published rate based on standard labor time or actual technician time, so faster administrative work and better scheduling need not be contractually passed through immediately. Local quote comparison, fleet negotiation, parts transparency, warranty expectations, and competition cause some sharing at renewal or the next visit. Fuel and convenience prices are highly visible, but the estimate concerns the implemented benefit in the qualifying service.

caveats: No implementation study specific to gasoline-station repair businesses was found.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects and applies only to exposed tasks in a.
rho.change_evidence: A 12- to 24-month matched study of station-repair operators deploying AI, reporting labor hours, estimate accuracy, rework, safety incidents, shrink, customer complaints, and realized hiring avoidance by workflow, would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Within five years, operators can implement AI-assisted service intake, call handling, maintenance reminders, estimate explanations, parts lookup, diagnostic suggestions, scheduling, bookkeeping review, loss-prevention review, and inventory forecasting. Realization is constrained by legacy POS and shop systems, fragmented vehicle data, small-firm change capacity, liability, cybersecurity, false diagnoses, theft and exception handling, and the physical nature of repairs.

s5.bridge: Census measures the age of responding owners of employer businesses across all U.S. industries, not 457110 sellers or completed transfers. I use the 51% age-55-or-older share as succession-pressure evidence and assume only a minority becomes an external five-year control transfer after closures, family or internal succession, environmental diligence failures, franchise restrictions, real-estate separation, and non-LMM outcomes. Forecourt contamination and fuel-supply complexity likely make the all-business proxy overstate transferable-sale probability.
caveats: The owner-age source is all-industry and data year 2018.
No qualifying-transfer denominator for the narrow eligible subset was found.
A real-estate sale, fuel-brand change, or lease transfer is not necessarily a control transfer of the service operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed sales, closures, and failed processes for gasoline stations with repair bays from fuel-property brokers, SBA lending, environmental diligence providers, and transaction databases, matched to owner age and control outcome, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner aging supports recurring sale opportunities, and repair customers, site traffic, fuel contracts, and real estate can be transferable. Tanks, environmental liability, franchise and supply agreements, low-margin product exposure, owner dependence, and mixed property/operating-company structures reduce the probability that an eligible firm completes a qualifying control transaction.

principal_driver: A repair-bearing station can use AI in customer and shop administration while retaining indispensable physical service work.
principal_weakness: Substantive outsourced repair is optional and probably rare in this product-retail NAICS, leaving a very small eligible population.
